title: China Is Ready to Get Tough
gist: China has shifted from largely rhetorical protests to active, calibrated retaliation against perceived offenses (US tech/military-list sanctions, Japan's Taiwan remarks, Panama's port moves), a change the author attributes to China's accumulated economic and supply-chain leverage rather than a change in ideology.

themes.theme: China's retaliation doctrine has shifted from rhetoric to action

insights.claim: China's diplomatic posture has shifted from issuing protests and reserving the right to act, to actually implementing concrete countermeasures such as export bans and sanctions, marking a qualitative change from prior decades when Beijing mostly avoided real retaliation out of fear of damaging bilateral relations.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: Investors and companies operating in China-exposed supply chains should price in a materially higher probability of Beijing following through on retaliatory measures rather than assuming protests are just posturing.
insights.quote: Of late, though, China has been more willing to bare its fangs. It no longer limits itself to verbal protests without taking action.
insights.locator: Of late, though, China has been more willing to bare its fangs

insights.claim: In June 2026, Beijing banned exports of dual-use items to 10 U.S. companies in direct retaliation for Washington's expansion of its military-linked-enterprises list targeting leading Chinese companies, exemplifying a tit-for-tat sanctions cycle now defining U.S.-China relations.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: Expect continued reciprocal escalation in export-control lists between the US and China; companies on either country's target lists face rising deal and supply-chain risk.
insights.locator: The latest example is Beijing's announcement last month

insights.claim: China's operating rule for retaliation is now explicitly reciprocal and symmetric: if the US sanctions Chinese companies it sanctions US companies, and if the US restricts Chinese access to technology, China restricts US access to rare earths.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: Rare-earth and critical-mineral access should be treated as a live retaliatory lever China will pull whenever it faces new US tech restrictions, reinforcing the case for Western supply-chain diversification away from Chinese rare earths.
insights.quote: If you sanction my companies, I sanction yours; if you restrict my access to technology, I restrict your access to rare earths.
insights.locator: This has been especially true toward the United States

themes.theme: Accumulated economic leverage is the enabling mechanism

insights.claim: China's newfound willingness to retaliate is rooted in real structural leverage it did not previously have: the world's second-largest economy, a central position in global manufacturing supply chains, control over rare earths and critical materials, and industrial leadership in EVs, photovoltaics, batteries, drones, ports, and telecom equipment.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: China's retaliatory capacity is now backed by tangible industrial chokepoints (EVs, solar, batteries, drones, telecom, ports) — sector-specific exposure to these Chinese-dominated industries should be treated as geopolitical risk, not just competitive risk.
insights.locator: Today is different. China has the world's second-largest economy

insights.claim: Beijing has built a legal architecture to formalize retaliation, including the Anti-Foreign Sanctions Law, the Export Control Law, and the Unreliable Entity List, giving it institutionalized tools it lacked in earlier standoffs.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: Foreign firms and individuals should assume China now has ready-made legal instruments to sanction them quickly, not just informal administrative pressure.
insights.locator: legal tools such as the Anti-Foreign Sanctions Law

insights.claim: Beijing believes the U.S.-led international order is destabilizing and that the West can no longer unilaterally set the rules of international politics, viewing China's own rise as part of that structural power shift and as license to abandon low-profile, endurance-based diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: China's more assertive posture is framed internally as structural and durable rather than tactical, implying investors should not expect a reversion to the old low-profile diplomatic stance even under different Chinese leadership.
insights.locator: In Beijing's view, the global balance of power is shifting

themes.theme: Deterrence logic: retaliation as signaling, calibrated by target

insights.claim: China's leadership views non-retaliation as strategically costly because failing to respond to one round of sanctions signals to adversaries that pressuring China carries no cost, inviting further escalation; this deterrence logic is now a core driver of Chinese retaliatory behavior.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: Expect China to respond to essentially every major sanction or restriction going forward as a matter of signaling discipline, not case-by-case cost-benefit analysis — reduces predictability of any single de-escalation.
insights.locator: Beijing sees containment by the outside world

insights.claim: China calibrates retaliation by target strength: against the U.S. it has used near-comprehensive tools (sanctions on defense contractors, members of Congress, think tanks), but against weaker Global South states like Panama it uses more restrained pressure (port inspections, ship detentions, trade friction) rather than military action or full diplomatic rupture, in a dispute over Chinese firms' operating rights at Panama Canal ports.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: Smaller, weaker states in disputes with Beijing should expect proportionate economic/administrative pressure rather than existential rupture, a distinct risk profile from China's treatment of the US or Japan.
insights.locator: Look at Panama. Over the issue of operating rights

insights.claim: Against Japan, after PM Sanae Takaichi suggested in November 2025 that Japan might defend Taiwan militarily and then refused to retract the remarks, China escalated from diplomatic protests to travel restrictions to near-comprehensive sanctions and a freeze of official exchanges, showing China will impose maximal pressure on mid-sized powers it judges have crossed a core-interest red line.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: Japan-China relations are a live flashpoint with real economic sanction risk; firms with Japan-China cross-exposure should monitor Taiwan-related statements from Japanese officials as a sanctions trigger.
insights.locator: Take Japan. Since Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi

insights.claim: The author argues China's more aggressive retaliation carries a self-defeating second-order risk: the more China uses rare-earth controls, market-access denial, and gray-zone maritime pressure, the faster the U.S., Japan, and Europe will build supply chains independent of China, multinationals will diversify away, and Southeast Asian states will accept expanded U.S. security presence.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: China's own retaliatory playbook is likely to accelerate exactly the supply-chain de-risking and alliance-building trends (China+1, ex-China rare earth projects, expanded US basing in Southeast Asia) that investors are already pricing into diversification themes.
insights.quote: Realism can create deterrence, but it can also create counterdeterrence.
insights.locator: This diplomacy has changed the outside world's expectations of China

insights.claim: The author frames the strategic challenge for Beijing as finding restraint within toughness — making the other side feel pain without hardening its resolve — describing this balance as the mark of mature great-power realism, implying current Chinese policy has not yet consistently achieved it.
insights.speaker: Deng Yuwen
insights.so_what: Watch whether Chinese retaliation episodes escalate past proportional pain into rupture-triggering territory as a leading indicator of whether Beijing is exercising the 'restrained realism' the author says is still unproven.
insights.quote: Force is not difficult. What is difficult is a strategic and restrained realism: Making retaliation serve strategy, rather than allowing strategy to serve retaliation.
insights.locator: If China simply grows tougher in its attitudes
